ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef brisket food product entry; no specific tomato-braised variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific tomato braised beef brisket entry. A00LC (bovine meat cuts) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for this prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Tomato Beef Brisket is approximately 300 g, comprising braised beef brisket pieces in tomato-based sauce, often served with steamed rice or noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, brisket, flat half, separable lean and fat, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 13325); adapted with tomato sauce component using USDA FoodData Central tomato-based sauce reference
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros derived from USDA SR Legacy braised beef brisket (NDB 13325) as the dominant component (~70% by weight). Tomato sauce contribution (~30% by weight) estimated using USDA FoodData Central tomato sauce reference (FDC ID 170457). Weighted blend applied per 100 g of finished dish. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce seasoning typical of Chinese-style preparation, consistent with HPB Singapore nutrient data for similar braised beef dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; beef and low-carbohydrate braised meat dishes reference; tomato-based stew analogue
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tomato Beef Brisket. GI estimated at 25 based on analogous braised beef and tomato stew dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Beef brisket is a protein- and fat-dominant food with minimal carbohydrate; tomato contributes low-GI carbohydrates (GI ~15–30 for tomatoes). Weighted estimate of GI 25 is defensible and conservative. GL per 300 g serving = (25 × 4.8 × 3) / 100 = 3.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less per meal, as a 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, which represents over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tomato Beef Brisket has a low GI of approximately 25 and provides only 4.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and cholesterol from beef brisket, and high sodium from soy-based braising
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of this dish due to its saturated fat content (~3.4 g/100 g) and high sodium load; choosing leaner cuts or smaller portions and pairing with vegetables can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato Beef Brisket (番茄牛腩, Fānqié niúnǎn) is a classic Chinese braised dish in which beef brisket is slow-cooked with fresh or canned tomatoes, soy sauce, ginger, garlic, and aromatics until the meat is tender and the sauce is rich and slightly tangy. The dish is popular across mainland China, Hong Kong, and among Chinese communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The brisket, a collagen-rich cut from the lower chest of the cow, becomes gelatinous and fork-tender after prolonged braising, yielding a deeply savoury, mildly sweet, and umami-forward flavour profile. The tomato component provides acidity that balances the richness of the beef fat and adds lycopene and vitamin C. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of protein (~15.8 g/100 g), iron (~2.1 mg/100 g), zinc (~4.2 mg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (~1.8 mcg/100 g). It has a low glycemic index (~25) due to its dominant protein and fat macronutrient profile, making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management. However, sodium content is elevated (~420 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce seasoning, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is commonly served over steamed white rice or with noodles in restaurant settings. In Singapore, it appears on the menus of Chinese restaurants and zi char stalls, and is a staple comfort food in Cantonese and Northern Chinese culinary traditions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tomato beef brisket?
Is tomato beef brisket suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of braised beef brisket?
Can I eat tomato beef brisket if I have high blood pressure?
Is beef brisket high in fat?
What nutrients does tomato beef brisket provide?

confidence_reason: No exact match for Tomato Beef Brisket found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy braised beef brisket (NDB 13325) as the primary component, blended with USDA tomato sauce reference values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is transparent, the dominant ingredient (braised beef brisket) has strong USDA primary source support, and the tomato component is well-characterised.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for braised beef brisket and is not reliably present in tomato-based sauces; no defensible value can be assigned. All other -1 fields in per_100g are populated with estimated values from the USDA source blend. Sodium value of 420 mg/100 g reflects soy sauce seasoning typical of Chinese-style braised beef; this is an upward adjustment from the base USDA braised brisket value (~65 mg/100 g) to account for soy sauce contribution, consistent with HPB Singapore data for similar braised Chinese beef dishes. Halal status is marked false as pork-derived ingredients or cross-contamination cannot be excluded in a restaurant context without certification confirmation. The dish is served at Bei Jiang Restaurant 北疆饭店 (Boat Quay, Singapore) per field survey source data; price SGD 33.93 per order. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this specific dish has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of the processing date.
